stem: The nurse is reviewing the electronic health record for a 55-year-old male client in the emergency department for acute chest pain. Based on the provided clinical data, identify the condition the client is most likely experiencing, the actions to take, and parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: History & Physical
tabs.content:
Patient: John Smith, 55-year-old male.

Chief Complaint: Crushing substernal chest pain, 9/10, radiating to left jaw, started 1 hour ago.

History of Present Illness: Patient was watching television when he experienced sudden onset of severe chest pain. Describes it as 'an elephant on my chest.' Associated diaphoresis and nausea. No relief from one sublingual nitroglycerin taken at home.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia.

Home Medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Atorvastatin 40 mg daily.

tabs.title: Nurse's Note
tabs.content:
SBAR Report (ED Nurse to Cardiology)

Situation: This is the ED nurse with an update on John Smith, a 55-year-old male who presented at 08:15 with crushing substernal chest pain, 9/10. 

Background: He has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. One home nitroglycerin was ineffective. 

Assessment: At 08:30, BP is 155/92, HR 102, SpO2 94%. He is diaphoretic. The 12-lead ECG shows ST-elevation in V2-V4, consistent with an anteroseptal STEMI. 

Recommendation: Aspirin 325 mg has been given. We need to activate the cardiac cath lab immediately.

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
Time: 08:30

Blood Pressure: 155/92 mmHg
Heart Rate: 102/min
Respiratory Rate: 22/min
SpO2: 94% on room air
Temperature: 37.1°C (98.8°F)

tabs.title: Diagnostics
tabs.content:
12-Lead ECG (08:25):
- Rhythm: Sinus Tachycardia
- Findings: ST-segment elevation of 3 mm in leads V2, V3, and V4.
- Interpretation: Findings consistent with acute anteroseptal ST-elevation myocardial infarction.

tabs.title: Laboratory Results
tabs.content:
Time: 08:45

Troponin I: 0.5 ng/mL (Reference range: <0.04 ng/mL)
CK-MB: Pending
Complete Blood Count (CBC): Within normal limits
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (CMP): Within normal limits

tabs.title: Orders
tabs.content:
Time: 08:30
Provider: Dr. M. Chen

- MONA protocol for chest pain
- Oxygen to maintain SpO2 >94%
- Aspirin 325 mg, chewable, STAT
- Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL q5min x3 PRN chest pain, hold for SBP <90
- Establish two large-bore IV lines
- STAT 12-Lead ECG
- STAT Troponin I, CK-MB, CBC, CMP

actions.text: Apply heat packs to the chest wall.

hoverRationales.cond1: This is a clinical flaw. Unstable angina involves myocardial ischemia but typically does not cause ST-segment elevation, which indicates complete coronary occlusion and transmural infarction.
hoverRationales.cond2: This is a clinical flaw. Aortic dissection often presents with tearing chest pain radiating to the back and may show a widened mediastinum on imaging, neither of which are present here.
hoverRationales.cond3: This is a clinical flaw. Pericarditis typically causes diffuse, concave ST-segment elevation across multiple lead territories, unlike the localized elevation seen in this client's ECG.
hoverRationales.cond4: This addresses the pathophysiology. A STEMI results from a complete coronary artery occlusion by a thrombus, leading to transmural myocardial ischemia and necrosis. This process causes the characteristic ST-segment elevation on the ECG.
hoverRationales.action1: This is a clinical flaw. Heat packs are used for musculoskeletal pain and have no therapeutic effect on ischemic cardiac pain, which is caused by myocardial oxygen deprivation.
hoverRationales.action2: This addresses the pathophysiology. Emergent cardiac catheterization with percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) is the definitive treatment to mechanically restore blood flow to the occluded coronary artery, preserving myocardial tissue.
hoverRationales.action3: This addresses the pathophysiology. Nitroglycerin is a vasodilator that reduces myocardial oxygen demand by decreasing preload. It also increases myocardial oxygen supply by dilating coronary arteries, helping to alleviate ischemic chest pain.
hoverRationales.action4: This is a clinical flaw. NSAIDs like ketorolac are contraindicated in suspected MI as they increase the risk of mortality, reinfarction, and heart failure by impairing myocardial healing.
hoverRationales.param1: This addresses the pathophysiology. Troponin and CK-MB are cardiac-specific enzymes released into the bloodstream during myocardial cell necrosis. Monitoring their levels confirms the diagnosis and quantifies the extent of heart muscle damage.
hoverRationales.param2: This is a clinical flaw. Abdominal circumference is a measure of visceral adiposity and a risk factor for heart disease, but it is irrelevant in the acute management of a myocardial infarction.
hoverRationales.param3: This addresses the pathophysiology. Continuous ECG monitoring is vital to detect and manage life-threatening arrhythmias, such as ventricular fibrillation, which are common and deadly complications of acute myocardial ischemia.
hoverRationales.param4: This is a clinical flaw. While important for baseline assessment, renal function labs are not the priority for diagnosing or managing the immediate life-threatening aspects of an acute STEMI.
